title: PDR COURT Mediation Rules for effective settlements
description: PDR COURT Mediation Rules are formulated to resolve civil & commercial disputes between the parties in a fair, efficient, harmonious & cost-effective manner
excerpt: PDR COURT Mediation Rules are formulated to resolve civil & commercial disputes between the parties in a fair, efficient, harmonious & cost-effective manner

blocks.text: Mediation Rules
Got any Questions

blocks.html: Write to us
blocks.text: Write to us
Chapter 1: General Provisions

blocks.html: These rules are formulated to resolve civil and commercial disputes between the parties by Mediation in a fair, efficient, harmonious and cost-effective manner.
blocks.text: These rules are formulated to resolve civil and commercial disputes between the parties by Mediation in a fair, efficient, harmonious and cost-effective manner.
Article 2 : Scope of Mediation Cases

blocks.html: Any civil and commercial dispute between natural persons, legal entities and unincorporated organisations as equal parties may be submitted to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED for Mediation.
blocks.text: Any civil and commercial dispute between natural persons, legal entities and unincorporated organisations as equal parties may be submitted to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED for Mediation.
Article 3 Scope of Application

blocks.html: Where the parties have agreed to refer their dispute to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED for Mediation, they shall be deemed to have agreed to mediation in accordance with these Rules.
blocks.text: Where the parties have agreed to refer their dispute to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED for Mediation, they shall be deemed to have agreed to mediation in accordance with these Rules.

blocks.html: Where the parties agree to refer their dispute to Mediation under these Rules, they shall be deemed to have agreed to have the Mediation procedure administered by PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED.
blocks.text: Where the parties agree to refer their dispute to Mediation under these Rules, they shall be deemed to have agreed to have the Mediation procedure administered by PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED.

blocks.html: Where the parties have agreed otherwise and with the approval of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED, the parties’ agreement shall prevail.
blocks.text: Where the parties have agreed otherwise and with the approval of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED, the parties’ agreement shall prevail.
Article 4 : Voluntary Mediation

blocks.html: Mediation shall follow the Principle of Voluntary Participation by the parties.
blocks.text: Mediation shall follow the Principle of Voluntary Participation by the parties.
Article 5 : Fair Mediation

blocks.html: The Mediator shall conduct mediation fairly. The Mediator shall not represent either party and shall remain independent and impartial and treat the parties equally.
blocks.text: The Mediator shall conduct mediation fairly. The Mediator shall not represent either party and shall remain independent and impartial and treat the parties equally.

blocks.html: The Mediator shall cooperate closely and diligently carry out his/her duties as a mediator. The Mediator shall not be negligent or inefficient.
blocks.text: The Mediator shall cooperate closely and diligently carry out his/her duties as a mediator. The Mediator shall not be negligent or inefficient.

blocks.html: The Mediator shall actively facilitate the communication and negotiation between the parties and assist them in reaching a Settlement Agreement.
blocks.text: The Mediator shall actively facilitate the communication and negotiation between the parties and assist them in reaching a Settlement Agreement.
Chapter 2: Mediation Proceedings
Article 6: Commencement of Mediation

blocks.html: The parties may unilaterally or jointly apply to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED for Mediation, with or without a Mediation Agreement previously concluded between the parties. The Mediation proceedings shall commence on the day when PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED receives a Request for Mediation.
blocks.text: The parties may unilaterally or jointly apply to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED for Mediation, with or without a Mediation Agreement previously concluded between the parties. The Mediation proceedings shall commence on the day when PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED receives a Request for Mediation.
Article 7 : Request for Mediation

blocks.html: When applying for Mediation under these Rules, any party or parties shall:
blocks.text: When applying for Mediation under these Rules, any party or parties shall:

items.html: submit a Request for Mediation in writing signed and/or sealed by the party or parties, which shall, inter alia, include: the parties’ names, addresses, telephone, email, or any other means of communication;
items.text: submit a Request for Mediation in writing signed and/or sealed by the party or parties, which shall, inter alia, include: the parties’ names, addresses, telephone, email, or any other means of communication;

items.html: a description of the dispute and the mediation request;
items.text: a description of the dispute and the mediation request;

items.html: submit parties’ identification documents and submit a Power of Attorney if the party authorises agent(s) to participate in the Mediation proceedings;
items.text: submit parties’ identification documents and submit a Power of Attorney if the party authorises agent(s) to participate in the Mediation proceedings;

items.html: prepay the registration fee for Mediation as per the Mediation Fee Schedule attached to these Rules.
items.text: prepay the registration fee for Mediation as per the Mediation Fee Schedule attached to these Rules.

blocks.html: The parties’ agreement to refer their dispute for Mediation in accordance with these Rules or by PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED (if any) shall be enclosed with the Request for Mediation along with the evidence and other documents upon which the requests are based. The party applying for Mediation may declare in writing that part or all of its evidence and other supporting documents be accessible to the Mediator only.
blocks.text: The parties’ agreement to refer their dispute for Mediation in accordance with these Rules or by PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED (if any) shall be enclosed with the Request for Mediation along with the evidence and other documents upon which the requests are based. The party applying for Mediation may declare in writing that part or all of its evidence and other supporting documents be accessible to the Mediator only.
Article 8 : Acceptance of a Case

blocks.html: Upon receipt of a Request for Mediation and its attachments, together with the identification documents of the parties, if PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED finds, after examination, the formalities required for the Mediation application to be complete, it shall accept the request and send a Notice of Mediation to both parties together with a copy of these Rules and the Panel of Mediators.
blocks.text: Upon receipt of a Request for Mediation and its attachments, together with the identification documents of the parties, if PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED finds, after examination, the formalities required for the Mediation application to be complete, it shall accept the request and send a Notice of Mediation to both parties together with a copy of these Rules and the Panel of Mediators.

blocks.html: Where after examination, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED finds the formalities required for the Mediation application to be incomplete, it may request the applying party or parties to complete or amend them within a specified time period.
blocks.text: Where after examination, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED finds the formalities required for the Mediation application to be incomplete, it may request the applying party or parties to complete or amend them within a specified time period.

blocks.html: After PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED accepts a case, it shall designate a Case Manager (CM) to assist with the procedural administration of the Mediation.
blocks.text: After PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED accepts a case, it shall designate a Case Manager (CM) to assist with the procedural administration of the Mediation.
Article 9 : Confirmation to Participate in the Mediation by the Other Party

blocks.html: The other party shall, within 15 days from the date of receipt of the Notice for Mediation, confirm in writing whether it agrees or declines to participate in the Mediation proceedings unless both parties have jointly applied for Mediation. Where the other party does have difficulty in giving its reply within the said time period, it may apply for an extension before the deadline.
blocks.text: The other party shall, within 15 days from the date of receipt of the Notice for Mediation, confirm in writing whether it agrees or declines to participate in the Mediation proceedings unless both parties have jointly applied for Mediation. Where the other party does have difficulty in giving its reply within the said time period, it may apply for an extension before the deadline.

blocks.html: If the other party expressly declines to participate in the Mediation proceedings or fails to give any response within the specified time period, the Mediation proceedings shall terminate.
blocks.text: If the other party expressly declines to participate in the Mediation proceedings or fails to give any response within the specified time period, the Mediation proceedings shall terminate.

blocks.html: Where the other party confirms in writing its participation in the Mediation after the specified deadline, the Mediation proceedings may continue with the consent of the party applying for Mediation.
blocks.text: Where the other party confirms in writing its participation in the Mediation after the specified deadline, the Mediation proceedings may continue with the consent of the party applying for Mediation.

blocks.html: Where the other party confirms in writing to participate in the Mediation proceedings, all parties may, with the assistance of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED, reach a consensus on matters such as the method of payment and the allocation of the mediation fee advance between the parties, the number of mediators and the approach of appointment and the place of mediation.
blocks.text: Where the other party confirms in writing to participate in the Mediation proceedings, all parties may, with the assistance of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED, reach a consensus on matters such as the method of payment and the allocation of the mediation fee advance between the parties, the number of mediators and the approach of appointment and the place of mediation.

blocks.html: Where the parties fail to agree on the method and allocation of the advance payment, all parties shall prepay an equal proportion of the mediation fee.
blocks.text: Where the parties fail to agree on the method and allocation of the advance payment, all parties shall prepay an equal proportion of the mediation fee.

blocks.html: Where a party agrees to participate in the Mediation but refuses to prepay its proportion of the mediation fee, the other party or parties may prepay all the mediation fee.
blocks.text: Where a party agrees to participate in the Mediation but refuses to prepay its proportion of the mediation fee, the other party or parties may prepay all the mediation fee.
Article 10 : Reply

blocks.html: The other party shall, within 20 days from the date of giving its written confirmation to participate in the Mediation proceedings, submit its reply (if any) in writing to the Request for Mediation as well as the facts and reasons. It may declare in writing that part or all of its evidence and other supporting documents be accessible to the Mediator only.
blocks.text: The other party shall, within 20 days from the date of giving its written confirmation to participate in the Mediation proceedings, submit its reply (if any) in writing to the Request for Mediation as well as the facts and reasons. It may declare in writing that part or all of its evidence and other supporting documents be accessible to the Mediator only.
Article 11 : Copies of Documents

blocks.html: The parties shall make their submissions in triplicate. Where there are more than two parties or more than one Mediator, additional copies shall be provided accordingly.
blocks.text: The parties shall make their submissions in triplicate. Where there are more than two parties or more than one Mediator, additional copies shall be provided accordingly.
Article 12 : Appointment of Mediator

blocks.html: The parties may agree on the number of Mediators and the approach of mediator appointment. Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the Mediation proceedings shall be conducted by a sole mediator.
blocks.text: The parties may agree on the number of Mediators and the approach of mediator appointment. Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the Mediation proceedings shall be conducted by a sole mediator.

blocks.html: The parties may jointly nominate a Mediator, exclusively from PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED’s Panel of Mediators.
blocks.text: The parties may jointly nominate a Mediator, exclusively from PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED’s Panel of Mediators.

blocks.html: Within 10 days from the date of the other party’s confirmation to participate in the Mediation, or within 10 days from the date of receipt of the Notice for Mediation in the case of a joint application by all the parties, the parties shall notify PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED of their joint nomination of the Mediator in writing or entrust PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED to appoint a Mediator for them.
blocks.text: Within 10 days from the date of the other party’s confirmation to participate in the Mediation, or within 10 days from the date of receipt of the Notice for Mediation in the case of a joint application by all the parties, the parties shall notify PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED of their joint nomination of the Mediator in writing or entrust PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED to appoint a Mediator for them.

blocks.html: Where the parties fail to jointly nominate or jointly entrust PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED to appoint a Mediator or fail to nominate a Mediator in accordance with their agreed approach within their agreed time period, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall appoint the Mediator for the parties.
blocks.text: Where the parties fail to jointly nominate or jointly entrust PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED to appoint a Mediator or fail to nominate a Mediator in accordance with their agreed approach within their agreed time period, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall appoint the Mediator for the parties.

blocks.html: If either party, within 10 days upon receipt of the Notice of the Mediator Appointment, raises any written objection to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED-appointed Mediator with reasons stated, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may appoint another Mediator.
blocks.text: If either party, within 10 days upon receipt of the Notice of the Mediator Appointment, raises any written objection to PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED-appointed Mediator with reasons stated, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may appoint another Mediator.
Article 13 : Mediator’s Acceptance of the Nomination or Appointment

blocks.html: The Mediator shall confirm whether to accept the nomination or appointment within 10 days upon receipt of the Notice of Nomination or Appointment from PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED.
blocks.text: The Mediator shall confirm whether to accept the nomination or appointment within 10 days upon receipt of the Notice of Nomination or Appointment from PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED.

blocks.html: If the Mediator declines the nomination or appointment or fails to reply within the above time period, a new Mediator shall be nominated or appointed according to the same procedure that applied to the nomination or appointment of the previous Mediator.
blocks.text: If the Mediator declines the nomination or appointment or fails to reply within the above time period, a new Mediator shall be nominated or appointed according to the same procedure that applied to the nomination or appointment of the previous Mediator.
Article 14 : Disclosure and Conflict of Interest

blocks.html: Within 10 days of acceptance of the nomination or appointment, the Mediator shall sign a Declaration of Independence and Impartiality and shall disclose in writing if there exist any circumstances likely to give rise to justifiable doubts as to his/her impartiality or independence. If circumstances that need to be disclosed arise during the Mediation proceedings, the Mediator shall disclose such circumstances to the parties without undue delay. After the disclosure, with the approval of all parties, the Mediator may continue to carry out his/her duties to mediate.
blocks.text: Within 10 days of acceptance of the nomination or appointment, the Mediator shall sign a Declaration of Independence and Impartiality and shall disclose in writing if there exist any circumstances likely to give rise to justifiable doubts as to his/her impartiality or independence. If circumstances that need to be disclosed arise during the Mediation proceedings, the Mediator shall disclose such circumstances to the parties without undue delay. After the disclosure, with the approval of all parties, the Mediator may continue to carry out his/her duties to mediate.
Article 15 : Replacement of the Mediator

blocks.html: If it is unfeasible or inappropriate for a Mediator to continue to fulfill his/her duties as a Mediator, a new Mediator shall be determined according to Article 12 of these Rules unless the parties have agreed otherwise.
blocks.text: If it is unfeasible or inappropriate for a Mediator to continue to fulfill his/her duties as a Mediator, a new Mediator shall be determined according to Article 12 of these Rules unless the parties have agreed otherwise.
Article 16 : Confidentiality

blocks.html: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, Mediation shall be conducted in camera with no written record of the mediation process.
blocks.text: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, Mediation shall be conducted in camera with no written record of the mediation process.

blocks.html: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties or stipulated in the relevant law or administrative regulations, the Mediator(s), the parties and their authorised representatives, other participants to the Mediation proceedings, and the staff members of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall not disclose to any outsider any substantive or procedural matters relating to the case.
blocks.text: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties or stipulated in the relevant law or administrative regulations, the Mediator(s), the parties and their authorised representatives, other participants to the Mediation proceedings, and the staff members of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall not disclose to any outsider any substantive or procedural matters relating to the case.
Article 17 : Conduct of Mediation

blocks.html: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, a Mediator may conduct the Mediation in any way he or she deems appropriate, including but not limited to:
blocks.text: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, a Mediator may conduct the Mediation in any way he or she deems appropriate, including but not limited to:

items.html: meeting with the parties or their authorised representatives separately or concurrently, and if necessary, after soliciting the opinions of the parties, the Mediator may decide to meet with the parties without the presence of their agents;
items.text: meeting with the parties or their authorised representatives separately or concurrently, and if necessary, after soliciting the opinions of the parties, the Mediator may decide to meet with the parties without the presence of their agents;

items.html: requesting the parties to provide supplemental materials or relevant opinions;
items.text: requesting the parties to provide supplemental materials or relevant opinions;

items.html: requesting the parties to put forward their oral or written settlement proposals;
items.text: requesting the parties to put forward their oral or written settlement proposals;

items.html: hearing witness statement(s);
items.text: hearing witness statement(s);

items.html: conducting an on-site examination or engaging relevant experts to provide consulting advice or an appraisal opinion with respect to professional or technical issues, with the approval of all parties;
items.text: conducting an on-site examination or engaging relevant experts to provide consulting advice or an appraisal opinion with respect to professional or technical issues, with the approval of all parties;

items.html: providing suggestions and proposals to settle the dispute.
items.text: providing suggestions and proposals to settle the dispute.

blocks.text: Article 18 : Place of Mediation

blocks.html: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the Mediation proceeding shall be conducted Electronically through e-mail and video hearing.
blocks.text: Unless otherwise agreed by the parties, the Mediation proceeding shall be conducted Electronically through e-mail and video hearing.

blocks.html: Where the parties have agreed on the place of Mediation, the parties’ agreement shall prevail. Absence of such agreement, the Mediation shall be conducted in Mumbai at the Arbitration Centre of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED. If necessary, with the approval of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED, the Mediation may be conducted in other places as well.
blocks.text: Where the parties have agreed on the place of Mediation, the parties’ agreement shall prevail. Absence of such agreement, the Mediation shall be conducted in Mumbai at the Arbitration Centre of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED. If necessary, with the approval of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED, the Mediation may be conducted in other places as well.
Article 19 : Good Faith Cooperation

items.html: providing relevant documents, information and explanation as requested by the Mediator(s);
items.text: providing relevant documents, information and explanation as requested by the Mediator(s);

items.html: making best efforts to meet the requirement of the Mediator(s) to hear from witnesses or experts;
items.text: making best efforts to meet the requirement of the Mediator(s) to hear from witnesses or experts;

items.html: facilitating the on-site examination by the Meditator(s);
items.text: facilitating the on-site examination by the Meditator(s);

items.html: The Parties shall comply with any time limit agreed by themselves or decided by the Mediator(s).
items.text: The Parties shall comply with any time limit agreed by themselves or decided by the Mediator(s).

blocks.text: Article 20 : Settlement Agreement

blocks.html: The parties may reach a Settlement Agreement by themselves or with the assistance of the Mediator(s) at any stage of the Mediation proceedings. The Settlement Agreement shall, inter alia, include:
blocks.text: The parties may reach a Settlement Agreement by themselves or with the assistance of the Mediator(s) at any stage of the Mediation proceedings. The Settlement Agreement shall, inter alia, include:

items.html: the names and addresses of the parties;
items.text: the names and addresses of the parties;

items.html: the Mediator(s);
items.text: the Mediator(s);

items.html: the Mediation requests and the matters for Mediation;
items.text: the Mediation requests and the matters for Mediation;

items.html: the settlement result agreed upon by the parties;
items.text: the settlement result agreed upon by the parties;

items.html: the time and place of the conclusion of the settlement agreement.
items.text: the time and place of the conclusion of the settlement agreement.

blocks.html: After all the parties and the Mediator(s) have signed and/or stamped the Settlement Agreement, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall affix its seal on such agreement. The Settlement Agreement is binding upon all the parties.
blocks.text: After all the parties and the Mediator(s) have signed and/or stamped the Settlement Agreement, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall affix its seal on such agreement. The Settlement Agreement is binding upon all the parties.

blocks.html: The parties shall comply with and carry out the Settlement Agreement in good faith.
blocks.text: The parties shall comply with and carry out the Settlement Agreement in good faith.

blocks.html: The parties may reach a Settlement Agreement on part of the Mediation requests.
blocks.text: The parties may reach a Settlement Agreement on part of the Mediation requests.
Article 21 : Termination of the Mediation Proceedings

items.html: in the case of unilateral application, the other party expressly declines to participate in the Mediation proceedings or fails to give any response within the specified time period after receipt of the Notice for Mediation;
items.text: in the case of unilateral application, the other party expressly declines to participate in the Mediation proceedings or fails to give any response within the specified time period after receipt of the Notice for Mediation;

items.html: any party notifies in writing that the Mediation proceedings shall be terminated;
items.text: any party notifies in writing that the Mediation proceedings shall be terminated;

items.html: at any stage of the Mediation proceedings, if in the view of the Mediator(s,) the parties are unlikely to reach a Settlement Agreement, after soliciting the opinions of the parties, the Mediator(s) may notify the parties in writing to terminate the Mediation proceedings;
items.text: at any stage of the Mediation proceedings, if in the view of the Mediator(s,) the parties are unlikely to reach a Settlement Agreement, after soliciting the opinions of the parties, the Mediator(s) may notify the parties in writing to terminate the Mediation proceedings;

items.html: the parties have reached a Settlement Agreement;
items.text: the parties have reached a Settlement Agreement;

items.html: the parties fail to pay the full amount of the Mediation fee;
items.text: the parties fail to pay the full amount of the Mediation fee;

items.html: other circumstances arise that require the termination of the Mediation proceedings.
items.text: other circumstances arise that require the termination of the Mediation proceedings.

blocks.text: Chapter 3 : Supplementary Provisions
Article 22 : Mediation Fee and Actual Expenses

blocks.html: The parties shall pay the Mediation fee according to the relevant provisions of these Rules and the Fee Schedule. The Fee Schedule shall form a part of these Rules.
blocks.text: The parties shall pay the Mediation fee according to the relevant provisions of these Rules and the Fee Schedule. The Fee Schedule shall form a part of these Rules.

blocks.html: The Mediation fee is composed of the Registration fee, the Administrative fee and the Mediator’s fee. PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may also charge the parties for other additional and reasonable actual expenses, including but not limited to the travel and accommodation expenses of the Mediator(s) and the Case Manager incurred in conducting the Mediation at a place other than the arbitration center of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED or for on-site examination, as well as the costs and expenses of the expert or appraiser appointed by the Mediator(s).
blocks.text: The Mediation fee is composed of the Registration fee, the Administrative fee and the Mediator’s fee. PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may also charge the parties for other additional and reasonable actual expenses, including but not limited to the travel and accommodation expenses of the Mediator(s) and the Case Manager incurred in conducting the Mediation at a place other than the arbitration center of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED or for on-site examination, as well as the costs and expenses of the expert or appraiser appointed by the Mediator(s).

blocks.html: Unless otherwise stipulated in these Rules or agreed by the parties, the aforesaid fees shall be borne by the parties on an equal basis.
blocks.text: Unless otherwise stipulated in these Rules or agreed by the parties, the aforesaid fees shall be borne by the parties on an equal basis.

blocks.html: Where the parties and the Mediator(s) have reached a special agreement on the Mediator’s fee, their agreement shall prevail.
blocks.text: Where the parties and the Mediator(s) have reached a special agreement on the Mediator’s fee, their agreement shall prevail.
Article 23 : Other Issues

blocks.html: Neither party may invoke any statement, view, opinion or proposal by the other party or by the Mediator(s) in the process of Mediation as grounds for any claim, defence or counterclaim in any arbitral proceedings, judicial proceedings or any other proceedings for the same or relevant dispute.
blocks.text: Neither party may invoke any statement, view, opinion or proposal by the other party or by the Mediator(s) in the process of Mediation as grounds for any claim, defence or counterclaim in any arbitral proceedings, judicial proceedings or any other proceedings for the same or relevant dispute.
Article 24 : Coordination of Mediation and Arbitration Mechanisms

blocks.html: Either party may, based on the arbitration clause contained in the Settlement Agreement or concluded between the parties separately, request PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED to render an arbitral award in accordance with the terms of the Settlement Agreement pursuant to the relevant provisions of its Arbitration Rules.
blocks.text: Either party may, based on the arbitration clause contained in the Settlement Agreement or concluded between the parties separately, request PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED to render an arbitral award in accordance with the terms of the Settlement Agreement pursuant to the relevant provisions of its Arbitration Rules.

blocks.html: Where the parties, pursuant to the relevant provision of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED’s Arbitration Rules, request an independent Mediation to be conducted by a Mediator different from the members of the arbitral tribunal during the arbitral proceedings of a PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED’s case, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall provide the corresponding Mediation service.
blocks.text: Where the parties, pursuant to the relevant provision of PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED’s Arbitration Rules, request an independent Mediation to be conducted by a Mediator different from the members of the arbitral tribunal during the arbitral proceedings of a PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED’s case, PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED shall provide the corresponding Mediation service.
Article 25 : Coordination of Mediation and Litigation Mechanisms

blocks.html: PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may provide the corresponding Mediation service with the entrustment or appointment by a court of law.
blocks.text: PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may provide the corresponding Mediation service with the entrustment or appointment by a court of law.
Article 26 : Other Supportive Services

blocks.html: PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may provide the corresponding services upon a request to appoint a Mediator and/or to provide other support services.
blocks.text: PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED may provide the corresponding services upon a request to appoint a Mediator and/or to provide other support services.
Article 27 : Interpretation

blocks.html: These Rules shall be interpreted by PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED.
blocks.text: These Rules shall be interpreted by PDR ORGANIZATION PRIVATE LIMITED.
Article 28 : Coming into Force

blocks.html: As a cycle manufacturer, we have a variety of partners and distributors that we deal with, and the market is totally demand-based, which, at times, is chaotic. The wide array of distributors spread over large geographical areas also brings its own challenges. Credits and deferred payment systems are an unavoidable part of the business, and ensuring efficient cash flow is a key focus area. Another factor they are aware of, and at certain times, advised us on, was to focus on the continuity of the business. With clients and vendors who had been historically nondelinquent, the suggestion that came up from the PDR COURT team was to be as lenient as possible and also find creative solutions so that the business relationship could be maintained and capitalized upon in the long run. This, we think, would not have happened if the case was approached by a typical legal expert.

Fact 1

blocks.html: A recent quantitative analysis by Economic Times estimates that approximately Rs 10.7 lakh crore is stuck as delayed payments to MSMEs in India, amounting to 6% of India’s GVA (Gross Value Added) for FY 2020-21. This report does indicate that though MSMEs have gained due importance and have enough legal and regulatory safeguards for delayed payments, still a lot needs to be done.
blocks.text: A recent quantitative analysis by Economic Times estimates that approximately Rs 10.7 lakh crore is stuck as delayed payments to MSMEs in India, amounting to 6% of India’s GVA (Gross Value Added) for FY 2020-21. This report does indicate that though MSMEs have gained due importance and have enough legal and regulatory safeguards for delayed payments, still a lot needs to be done.
Fact 2

blocks.html: Samadhan, (a platform launched by the government for MSMEs to raise disputes over the non-payment of dues) as of May 2022 had complaints raised by 1.08 lakh applications filed MSEs with delayed amounts totalling to Rs 28,085 crore since its launch in October 2017. A sure-shot indicator of the limitations of legal remedies.

Insight
‘’The Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSME) sector contributes one-third to India's gross domestic product and has the potential to grow further,’’
Minister Narayan Rane.

blocks.html: The MSME sector is evidently gaining priority in the economic development of the country. This sector not only represents the maximum workforce of the country but also contributes more than half of the country's output. With changes, challenges and obstacles faced in the business environment post-pandemic and the vividity of business opportunities, this sector needs all the support it can get to make sure that businesses focus on business continuity and growth.

title: NBFC NPA Issues: Faster Loan Recovery with PDR COURT
description: NBFCs face rising NPAs and delayed recoveries. Discover how PDR COURT offers a smarter, faster, and legally enforceable loan recovery solution.
excerpt: NBFCs face rising NPAs and delayed recoveries. Discover how PDR COURT offers a smarter, faster, and legally enforceable loan recovery solution.

blocks.text: Articles
NBFC NPA Issues: How PDR COURT Helps in Faster Loan Recovery
NBFCs face rising NPAs and delayed recoveries. Discover how PDR COURT offers a smarter, faster, and legally enforceable loan recovery solution.
NBFCs and the Growing NPA Problem

blocks.html: Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) are vital to India’s lending ecosystem, providing credit to individuals and businesses who often don’t have access to banks. But in recent years, NBFCs have faced a growing challenge—non-performing assets (NPAs).
blocks.text: Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) are vital to India’s lending ecosystem, providing credit to individuals and businesses who often don’t have access to banks. But in recent years, NBFCs have faced a growing challenge—non-performing assets (NPAs).

blocks.html: When borrowers fail to repay loans, NBFCs struggle with reduced liquidity and profitability. To address this, digital dispute resolution platforms like PDR COURT are emerging as powerful tools for NPA recovery in India.
blocks.text: When borrowers fail to repay loans, NBFCs struggle with reduced liquidity and profitability. To address this, digital dispute resolution platforms like PDR COURT are emerging as powerful tools for NPA recovery in India.
What Are NBFCs and Why Do They Face NPA Issues?

blocks.html: NBFCs are financial institutions that lend money but cannot accept demand deposits like banks. They focus on:
blocks.text: NBFCs are financial institutions that lend money but cannot accept demand deposits like banks. They focus on:

items.html: Retail lending (personal loans, vehicle loans, housing loans).
items.text: Retail lending (personal loans, vehicle loans, housing loans).

items.html: Small business and SME financing.
items.text: Small business and SME financing.

items.html: Microfinance in semi-urban and rural areas.
items.text: Microfinance in semi-urban and rural areas.

blocks.html: Their wide reach makes them critical for financial inclusion. But because they lend to high-risk segments, they face higher chances of defaults and loan recovery challenges.
blocks.text: Their wide reach makes them critical for financial inclusion. But because they lend to high-risk segments, they face higher chances of defaults and loan recovery challenges.
What Is an NPA (Non-Performing Asset)?

blocks.html: An NPA is a loan that has not been repaid (interest or principal) for more than 90 days.
blocks.text: An NPA is a loan that has not been repaid (interest or principal) for more than 90 days.

blocks.html: For NBFCs, NPAs:
blocks.text: For NBFCs, NPAs:

items.html: Reduce income and profitability.
items.text: Reduce income and profitability.

items.html: Block capital for fresh lending.
items.text: Block capital for fresh lending.

items.html: Hurt investor confidence and credit ratings.
items.text: Hurt investor confidence and credit ratings.

items.html: Invite stricter RBI regulations.
items.text: Invite stricter RBI regulations.

blocks.html: This makes NPA recovery a top priority for every NBFC.
blocks.text: This makes NPA recovery a top priority for every NBFC.
Challenges in NPA Recovery for NBFCs

items.html: Lengthy court cases : Civil suits and Debt Recovery Tribunals (DRTs) take years.
items.text: Lengthy court cases : Civil suits and Debt Recovery Tribunals (DRTs) take years.

items.html: High litigation costs : Legal fees and administrative costs reduce profitability.
items.text: High litigation costs : Legal fees and administrative costs reduce profitability.

items.html: Borrower resistance : Defaulters delay payments, knowing legal action is slow.
items.text: Borrower resistance : Defaulters delay payments, knowing legal action is slow.

items.html: Reputation risks : Aggressive recovery methods can damage brand image.
items.text: Reputation risks : Aggressive recovery methods can damage brand image.

blocks.html: Clearly, NBFCs need a faster, legally valid, and cost-effective recovery method.
blocks.text: Clearly, NBFCs need a faster, legally valid, and cost-effective recovery method.
Arbitration: A Smarter Way for Loan Recovery

blocks.html: Under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, arbitration is a legally recognized method of dispute resolution. It allows lenders and borrowers to settle disputes outside of court, with decisions (arbitration awards) being enforceable by law.
blocks.text: Under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, arbitration is a legally recognized method of dispute resolution. It allows lenders and borrowers to settle disputes outside of court, with decisions (arbitration awards) being enforceable by law.

blocks.html: This method saves both time and money compared to traditional litigation, making it ideal for NBFCs facing large numbers of NPAs.
blocks.text: This method saves both time and money compared to traditional litigation, making it ideal for NBFCs facing large numbers of NPAs.
PDR COURT: A Digital Solution for NBFC Loan Recovery

blocks.html: PDR COURT is an online arbitration and dispute resolution platform designed to help lenders and NBFCs recover dues quickly.
blocks.text: PDR COURT is an online arbitration and dispute resolution platform designed to help lenders and NBFCs recover dues quickly.

blocks.html: Key Features of PDR COURT for NBFCs:
blocks.text: Key Features of PDR COURT for NBFCs:

items.text: Filing : Easy initiation of cases within days.

items.text: Resolution – Improves cash flow by reducing delays.

blocks.text: Why PDR COURT Matters for India’s NBFC Sector

blocks.html: The Indian NBFC sector has already witnessed liquidity crises due to rising NPAs, most notably in 2018–19. Many institutions collapsed because they couldn’t recover dues in time.
blocks.text: The Indian NBFC sector has already witnessed liquidity crises due to rising NPAs, most notably in 2018–19. Many institutions collapsed because they couldn’t recover dues in time.

Conclusion: The Future of NBFC NPA Recovery

blocks.html: NBFCs are essential for India’s growth story, but NPAs remain their biggest challenge. Traditional recovery methods are slow and unsustainable in today’s fast-paced economy.

Rules at PDR COURT for Conducting Online/Traditional Dispute Resolution

blocks.html: These Rules shall be applicable to all parties who are signatories to the contract/ agreement document/link, which contains PDR COURT's Arbitration/Dispute Resolution Clause.
blocks.text: These Rules shall be applicable to all parties who are signatories to the contract/ agreement document/link, which contains PDR COURT's Arbitration/Dispute Resolution Clause.
Invocation of Dispute:

blocks.html: Any party to the contract, which contains PDR COURT's Dispute Resolution Clause, feels that a dispute has arisen between them and the other party to the contract shall invoke the Arbitration clause by visiting PDR COURT's website and by filling out the Dispute Resolution Form. The Disputant Party shall then pay administrative fees for availing of Dispute Resolution Services.
blocks.text: Any party to the contract, which contains PDR COURT's Dispute Resolution Clause, feels that a dispute has arisen between them and the other party to the contract shall invoke the Arbitration clause by visiting PDR COURT's website and by filling out the Dispute Resolution Form. The Disputant Party shall then pay administrative fees for availing of Dispute Resolution Services.
Pre-Arbitration Proceedings (Conciliation):

items.html: Before the appointment of an Arbitrator and setting up of an Arbitral Tribunal, PDR COURT professionals shall call both parties to resolve the dispute through Conciliation Proceedings.
items.text: Before the appointment of an Arbitrator and setting up of an Arbitral Tribunal, PDR COURT professionals shall call both parties to resolve the dispute through Conciliation Proceedings.

items.html: If the Respondent feels that the dispute can be resolved through Conciliation but at the same time disagrees with the amount claimed by the Claimant or feels that a certain amount has to be set off against the Claim, he/she/it can request for hearing of Conciliation or submit a written reply to PDR COURT.
items.text: If the Respondent feels that the dispute can be resolved through Conciliation but at the same time disagrees with the amount claimed by the Claimant or feels that a certain amount has to be set off against the Claim, he/she/it can request for hearing of Conciliation or submit a written reply to PDR COURT.

items.html: Before initiation of Conciliation Proceedings, the Respondent shall pay 30% of the Claim amount to the Claimant and Administrative Fees of PDR COURT as acceptance of Conciliation Proceedings within 15 Days from the date of receiving notification from PDR COURT regarding initiation of dispute resolution proceedings. The Respondent shall further fill in the settlement terms by visiting PDR COURT’s website.
items.text: Before initiation of Conciliation Proceedings, the Respondent shall pay 30% of the Claim amount to the Claimant and Administrative Fees of PDR COURT as acceptance of Conciliation Proceedings within 15 Days from the date of receiving notification from PDR COURT regarding initiation of dispute resolution proceedings. The Respondent shall further fill in the settlement terms by visiting PDR COURT’s website.

items.html: If the Respondent denies participating in Conciliation proceedings or fails to reply to the Conciliation invitation notification within 10 days from the date on which such notification was sent by PDR COURT, then the matter will be referred to Arbitration by appointment of an Arbitrator or filing of Claim and Defence Statements.
items.text: If the Respondent denies participating in Conciliation proceedings or fails to reply to the Conciliation invitation notification within 10 days from the date on which such notification was sent by PDR COURT, then the matter will be referred to Arbitration by appointment of an Arbitrator or filing of Claim and Defence Statements.

items.html: If the Settlement terms/amount mentioned/payment scheduled mentioned by the Respondent is not acceptable to the Claimant, the Claimant can request for closure of Conciliation Proceedings, and then the matter will be referred to Arbitration by appointment of an Arbitrator or filing of Claim and Defense Statements.
items.text: If the Settlement terms/amount mentioned/payment scheduled mentioned by the Respondent is not acceptable to the Claimant, the Claimant can request for closure of Conciliation Proceedings, and then the matter will be referred to Arbitration by appointment of an Arbitrator or filing of Claim and Defense Statements.

blocks.text: Setting up of Arbitral Tribunal and Subsequent Proceedings:

items.html: If the matter is not resolved at the Pre-Arbitration stage, a Tribunal shall be formed as per the Rules of Arbitration of PDR COURT.
items.text: If the matter is not resolved at the Pre-Arbitration stage, a Tribunal shall be formed as per the Rules of Arbitration of PDR COURT.

items.html: Both parties have to share the Fees of Arbitration Proceedings equally.
items.text: Both parties have to share the Fees of Arbitration Proceedings equally.

items.html: Unless otherwise agreed by the Arbitrator, the proceedings shall be documents only Arbitration.
items.text: Unless otherwise agreed by the Arbitrator, the proceedings shall be documents only Arbitration.

items.html: The Arbitrator can be appointed by PDR COURT either before or after receiving documents.
items.text: The Arbitrator can be appointed by PDR COURT either before or after receiving documents.

items.html: The Claimant will be given a period of 10 days to file the Statement of Claim along with the proof of service to the Respondent.
items.text: The Claimant will be given a period of 10 days to file the Statement of Claim along with the proof of service to the Respondent.

items.html: The Respondent will be given a period of 10 days from the date when he receives the Statement of Claim from the Claimant to file the Statement of Defence.
items.text: The Respondent will be given a period of 10 days from the date when he receives the Statement of Claim from the Claimant to file the Statement of Defence.

items.html: The Award shall be passed within 30 days from the receipt of the Statement of Defence.
items.text: The Award shall be passed within 30 days from the receipt of the Statement of Defence.

items.html: If the Respondent fails to file the Statement of Defence within 10 days from the date of receiving the Statement of Claim, the matter shall proceed ex-parte for Award.
items.text: If the Respondent fails to file the Statement of Defence within 10 days from the date of receiving the Statement of Claim, the matter shall proceed ex-parte for Award.

items.html: If the parties default in payment of Arbitration Fees, the matter shall stand dismissed.
items.text: If the parties default in payment of Arbitration Fees, the matter shall stand dismissed.

What Happens After an Arbitral Award? Finality, Enforcement & Challenges Explained
ATP article - 8

blocks.html: The arbitral award is often seen as the conclusive endpoint of a dispute resolution journey. However, in reality, the issuance of an award marks the beginning of a new legal and procedural phase—enforcement. Understanding what follows after an arbitral award is crucial, especially for businesses, financial institutions, and legal professionals who rely on arbitration to resolve commercial disputes.
blocks.text: The arbitral award is often seen as the conclusive endpoint of a dispute resolution journey. However, in reality, the issuance of an award marks the beginning of a new legal and procedural phase—enforcement. Understanding what follows after an arbitral award is crucial, especially for businesses, financial institutions, and legal professionals who rely on arbitration to resolve commercial disputes.

blocks.html: In this article, we explore the legal standing of an arbitral award in India, examine whether it is final, how it can be enforced, and what recourse remains if the losing party refuses to comply or challenges the award.
blocks.text: In this article, we explore the legal standing of an arbitral award in India, examine whether it is final, how it can be enforced, and what recourse remains if the losing party refuses to comply or challenges the award.
Is the Arbitral Award Final and Binding?

blocks.html: Under Section 35 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, an arbitral award is final and binding on the parties and any persons claiming under them. This means that once an award is rendered, it has the same effect as a court decree, subject to any valid legal challenge that may be filed under Section 34 of the Act.
blocks.text: Under Section 35 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, an arbitral award is final and binding on the parties and any persons claiming under them. This means that once an award is rendered, it has the same effect as a court decree, subject to any valid legal challenge that may be filed under Section 34 of the Act.

blocks.html: However, finality in law often includes contingencies. While the award is deemed final, Indian jurisprudence acknowledges that parties have the right to challenge it on limited grounds, such as fraud, bias, lack of jurisdiction, or violation of public policy. These narrow exceptions ensure that the sanctity of the arbitration process is preserved while allowing judicial oversight.
blocks.text: However, finality in law often includes contingencies. While the award is deemed final, Indian jurisprudence acknowledges that parties have the right to challenge it on limited grounds, such as fraud, bias, lack of jurisdiction, or violation of public policy. These narrow exceptions ensure that the sanctity of the arbitration process is preserved while allowing judicial oversight.
Enforcing the Award: From Paper to Payment

blocks.html: Finality holds little weight without enforceability. This is where Section 36 of the Act comes into play. Once the statutory period for challenging the award under Section 34 has expired—or if such a challenge has been dismissed—the award holder can initiate execution proceedings, just like a civil court decree.
blocks.text: Finality holds little weight without enforceability. This is where Section 36 of the Act comes into play. Once the statutory period for challenging the award under Section 34 has expired—or if such a challenge has been dismissed—the award holder can initiate execution proceedings, just like a civil court decree.

blocks.html: Recent amendments, especially the 2015 and 2019 Amendments, have significantly streamlined the enforcement framework. Before 2015, merely filing a Section 34 petition would result in an automatic stay on enforcement. However, now, a stay must be specifically granted by the court, and that too only upon conditions such as furnishing security. This change was a game-changer for creditors and businesses seeking timely enforcement.
blocks.text: Recent amendments, especially the 2015 and 2019 Amendments, have significantly streamlined the enforcement framework. Before 2015, merely filing a Section 34 petition would result in an automatic stay on enforcement. However, now, a stay must be specifically granted by the court, and that too only upon conditions such as furnishing security. This change was a game-changer for creditors and businesses seeking timely enforcement.

blocks.html: Importantly, enforcement is initiated before a competent civil court with jurisdiction. This typically depends on the seat of arbitration or where the losing party resides or holds assets. The Commercial Courts Act, 2015, has also accelerated these proceedings, ensuring time-bound disposal of execution matters in high-stake cases.
blocks.text: Importantly, enforcement is initiated before a competent civil court with jurisdiction. This typically depends on the seat of arbitration or where the losing party resides or holds assets. The Commercial Courts Act, 2015, has also accelerated these proceedings, ensuring time-bound disposal of execution matters in high-stake cases.
What If the Losing Party Ignores the Award?

blocks.html: A common question in arbitration is whether the respondent can simply choose to ignore the award. The short answer: not without consequences.
blocks.text: A common question in arbitration is whether the respondent can simply choose to ignore the award. The short answer: not without consequences.

blocks.html: Non-compliance can lead to execution proceedings, where courts may attach properties, freeze bank accounts, or even summon directors for willful default. Under Order XXI of the Civil Procedure Code, the award holder can pursue recovery like any other decree-holder in a civil suit.
blocks.text: Non-compliance can lead to execution proceedings, where courts may attach properties, freeze bank accounts, or even summon directors for willful default. Under Order XXI of the Civil Procedure Code, the award holder can pursue recovery like any other decree-holder in a civil suit.

blocks.html: Moreover, deliberate defiance of an arbitral award can also invite penalties under the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971, especially when court directions are flouted post-execution.
blocks.text: Moreover, deliberate defiance of an arbitral award can also invite penalties under the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971, especially when court directions are flouted post-execution.

blocks.html: That said, there are tactical delays that a respondent might employ—ranging from filing a Section 34 petition with a stay application, to engaging in parallel legal proceedings, especially in multi-jurisdictional disputes. This is why institutional and tech-backed platforms play a critical role in tracking post-award progress and reducing procedural hurdles.
blocks.text: That said, there are tactical delays that a respondent might employ—ranging from filing a Section 34 petition with a stay application, to engaging in parallel legal proceedings, especially in multi-jurisdictional disputes. This is why institutional and tech-backed platforms play a critical role in tracking post-award progress and reducing procedural hurdles.
Grounds for Challenge: Section 34 Explained

blocks.html: A losing party may attempt to set aside the award under Section 34, but this provision is not an appeal mechanism. The Supreme Court has reiterated time and again that courts cannot re-appreciate evidence or sit in judgment over the arbitrator’s reasoning.
blocks.text: A losing party may attempt to set aside the award under Section 34, but this provision is not an appeal mechanism. The Supreme Court has reiterated time and again that courts cannot re-appreciate evidence or sit in judgment over the arbitrator’s reasoning.

blocks.html: Permissible grounds include:
blocks.text: Permissible grounds include:
Incapacity of a party or invalid arbitration agreement
Violation of principles of natural justice (e.g., one party not heard)
Excess of jurisdiction by arbitrators
Award in conflict with the public policy of India
Fraud or corruption during proceedings

blocks.html: Even here, courts apply a high threshold of proof. The judiciary has repeatedly cautioned against misuse of Section 34 to delay or derail enforcement, thereby reinforcing the pro-arbitration stance of Indian courts post-2015.
blocks.text: Even here, courts apply a high threshold of proof. The judiciary has repeatedly cautioned against misuse of Section 34 to delay or derail enforcement, thereby reinforcing the pro-arbitration stance of Indian courts post-2015.
Practical Scenarios: What to Expect

blocks.html: In real-world scenarios, especially in debt recovery and commercial disputes, the post-award phase unfolds in layered stages:
blocks.text: In real-world scenarios, especially in debt recovery and commercial disputes, the post-award phase unfolds in layered stages:
Award Delivery: Sent to parties via registered post or digitally.

blocks.html: Cooling-Off Period: A statutory three-month period (extendable by 30 days) to challenge under Section 34.
blocks.text: Cooling-Off Period: A statutory three-month period (extendable by 30 days) to challenge under Section 34.

blocks.html: Execution Filing: If uncontested, the winner moves to enforcement under Section 36.
blocks.text: Execution Filing: If uncontested, the winner moves to enforcement under Section 36.

blocks.html: Stay Proceedings: If contested, the court may impose a conditional or absolute stay.
blocks.text: Stay Proceedings: If contested, the court may impose a conditional or absolute stay.

blocks.html: Asset Seizure or Settlement: Based on court orders, recovery is pursued or settlement negotiated.
blocks.text: Asset Seizure or Settlement: Based on court orders, recovery is pursued or settlement negotiated.

blocks.html: For lenders like banks and NBFCs, this clarity in enforcement is vital, especially in high-volume or non-performing asset (NPA) cases. Time-bound enforcement ensures better recovery rates and improves investor confidence in ADR mechanisms.
blocks.text: For lenders like banks and NBFCs, this clarity in enforcement is vital, especially in high-volume or non-performing asset (NPA) cases. Time-bound enforcement ensures better recovery rates and improves investor confidence in ADR mechanisms.
How Neutral ADR Platforms Like PDR COURT Help

blocks.html: PDR COURT, a neutral technology-driven ADR platform, facilitates end-to-end resolution while remaining legally compliant and unbiased. While it does not operate as an arbitral institution, it provides critical support across the post-award lifecycle—digital delivery of awards, follow-ups, guidance on enforcement procedures, and partner-led legal escalation if needed.
blocks.text: PDR COURT, a neutral technology-driven ADR platform, facilitates end-to-end resolution while remaining legally compliant and unbiased. While it does not operate as an arbitral institution, it provides critical support across the post-award lifecycle—digital delivery of awards, follow-ups, guidance on enforcement procedures, and partner-led legal escalation if needed.

blocks.html: Its platform-based model especially aids NBFCs, fintechs, and MSMEs who need cost-effective, enforceable resolutions. By reducing dependency on conventional legal channels, PDR COURT helps parties move swiftly from award to recovery.
blocks.text: Its platform-based model especially aids NBFCs, fintechs, and MSMEs who need cost-effective, enforceable resolutions. By reducing dependency on conventional legal channels, PDR COURT helps parties move swiftly from award to recovery.

blocks.html: Understanding what follows after an arbitral award is as important as knowing how to invoke arbitration in the first place. With the evolving legal landscape and a pro-enforcement stance from Indian courts, awards now have teeth—provided parties act swiftly and strategically. For institutions and businesses alike, this post-award clarity enhances the credibility and reliability of arbitration as a powerful tool for dispute resolution.
blocks.text: Understanding what follows after an arbitral award is as important as knowing how to invoke arbitration in the first place. With the evolving legal landscape and a pro-enforcement stance from Indian courts, awards now have teeth—provided parties act swiftly and strategically. For institutions and businesses alike, this post-award clarity enhances the credibility and reliability of arbitration as a powerful tool for dispute resolution.
